reasoning: The investigation began by analyzing the root cause ranked list, which identified a severe anomaly in the F1-c interface, specifically f1_c_packet_loss_dl_percentage, with a very low distance score, indicating a high probability of being the root cause. To verify the propagation, the graph tool was used to check the status of all interfaces and components. It was confirmed that F1-c, F1-u, E2, and A1 interfaces are all affected, as well as the O-DU, O-CU, Near-RT RIC, and Non-RT RIC components. Although multiple interfaces show issues, the severity of the hard error (packet loss) on the F1-c interface outweighs the volumetric and latency anomalies found on other interfaces. Causal precedence dictates that a failure in the F1-c control plane (connecting O-CU and O-DU) logically leads to the degradation of the F1-u user plane and subsequent reporting/control failures across the E2 and A1 interfaces.
actionable_steps: 1. Perform a physical and link-layer audit of the F1-c interface between the O-CU and O-DU to identify cable faults or port errors. 2. Check O-CU and O-DU logs for signaling errors or resource exhaustion that could lead to packet drops in the downlink. 3. Verify the F1-c configuration and MTU settings to ensure there is no fragmentation-related packet loss.
